The bas-reliefs of Angkor temples, such as those in Bayon, describe everyday life of the ancient Khmer kingdom, including scenes of palace life, naval battles on the river or lakes, and common scenes of the marketplace. Angkor Wat is constructed within a surrounding outer wall 1,024 m by 802 m and 4.5 m high. The outer wall is surrounded by a 30 m apron of open ground and  ...    five towers, four at four corners and one at the center. Unlike most Angkorian temples, Angkor Wat is oriented to the west. The typical western orientation, as explained by the scholars, was due to its dedication to Vishnu, who was associated with the west. title: Angkor Thom Capital City - The Angkor Guide
highlights: Thom. At the center of the Kingdom rises a Golden tower Bayon flanked by more than twenty lesser towers and several hundred  ...    Symbolically, Angkor Thom is a microcosm of the universe, divided into four parts by the main axes. The temple of the Bayon is situated at the exact center of the axes and stands as the symbolical link between heaven and earth. The wall  ...    The city of Angkor Thom consists of a square, each side of which is about three kilometers (1.9 miles) long a laterite  ...    The five entry towers are among the most photographed of all the ancient Cambodian ruins. Each sandstone tower rises 23 meters (75 feet) to the sky and is crowned with four heads, one facing each cardinal direction. The faces may represent the rulers of the four cardinal points at the summit of mount Meru.

title: Angkor Thom- Bayon, Baphuon and Iconic Terraces!
highlights: last capital of the Khmer empire, this royal palace and city was also built in a series of squares, with the Bayon temple at its center, Like Angkor Wat, this symbolized the universe with the center temple symbolizing Mt. Meru. The causeway across the moat is passable by car, and the bridge is decorated with gods on one side and demons on the other, both guarding the entrance to the city. The entry gates are 75 feet tall, with faces on top facing in each of the cardinal directions, perhaps symbolizing the gods on top of Mt. Meru. Our driver stopped  ...
